serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of provolone cheese is approximately 28g (one thin slice), commonly used as a sandwich filling, pizza topping, or eaten with charcuterie.

nutrient_source.title: Provolone Cheese — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Provolone cheese (cr_id F01010020). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for provolone cheese (FDC ID 173420) for validation; values are consistent. Extended micronutrient values (fatty acid fractions, vitamins, minerals) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173420.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — University of Sydney GI Database; provolone cheese not listed due to negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hard and semi-hard cheeses are not listed in the University of Sydney GI Database because their carbohydrate content is negligible and they do not produce a measurable glycemic response. GI is not applicable for provolone cheese. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate per 28g serving is approximately 0.6g, which is clinically negligible; GL = (null GI / 100) x 0.6g is treated as 0.0 for practical purposes.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 727mg per 100g, exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit provolone cheese to no more than 15–20g per occasion and use it as a flavour accent only; lower-sodium alternatives such as fresh mozzarella or ricotta are preferred for regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (2.14g/100g) and high protein (25.58g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but high saturated fat (~17.1g/100g) impairs insulin sensitivity with regular large-portion use
type2_diabetes.guidance: Provolone cheese is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes in small portions (20–30g) due to its negligible carbohydrate content, but saturated fat intake should be monitored and the cheese should be paired with high-fibre foods to optimise the overall meal glycaemic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~17.1g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol, and high sodium (727mg/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict provolone cheese to occasional small servings (15–20g) and prioritise lower-saturated-fat, lower-sodium dairy alternatives to reduce LDL cholesterol and blood pressure burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~17.1g/100g far exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high sodium also worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit provolone cheese to very small amounts (15–20g) as a flavour component only, and consider lower-fat dairy sources such as low-fat yogurt or ricotta for calcium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Provolone is a semi-hard Italian stretched-curd cheese (pasta filata) made from full-fat cow's milk, originating in southern Italy and now produced throughout the country. It is produced in two main styles: Provolone Dolce (mild, aged 2–3 months, smooth and buttery) and Provolone Piccante (sharp and piquant, aged 6 months or more). The cheese has a smooth, firm texture that melts well, making it popular for sandwiches, pizza, pasta bakes, and charcuterie boards. Nutritionally, provolone is energy-dense at 351 kcal per 100g, with high protein (25.58g/100g) and high fat (26.62g/100g, of which approximately 17.1g is saturated fat). Carbohydrate content is negligible at 2.14g/100g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Provolone is an excellent source of calcium (756mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (1.54mcg/100g), and provides meaningful amounts of phosphorus, zinc, and riboflavin. However, its very high sodium content (727mg/100g) makes it unsuitable as a primary food for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular health and rheumatoid arthritis management. Clinically, provolone is best consumed in small portions (15–30g) as a flavour component rather than a dietary staple.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in provolone cheese?
Is provolone cheese high in fat?
What is the difference between provolone dolce and provolone piccante?
Is provolone cheese good for high blood pressure?
How many calories in provolone cheese?
What cheese is similar to provolone?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01010020, confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for provolone cheese (FDC ID 173420). Extended micronutrient values (fatty acid profile, vitamins, minerals) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173420, which is an approved primary source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a semi-hard cow's milk cheese.
notes: Saturated fat (17.08g/100g), monounsaturated fat (7.24g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.76g/100g), and extended micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173420) to supplement HPB data which does not report fatty acid fractions or full vitamin/mineral panel. Trans fat is reported as 0.0g per USDA SR Legacy — confirmed trace/negligible. Dietary fibre is 0.0g — confirmed zero for this cheese type per both HPB and USDA sources. Vitamin C is 0.0g — naturally zero for aged hard/semi-hard cheese. Provolone cheese; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (2.14g/100g). GI set to null and gi_category set to 'not_applicable' per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 because carbohydrate per 28g serving is approximately 0.6g, which is clinically negligible. Halal status not confirmed — provolone may use animal rennet; consumers requiring halal-certified cheese should verify with the manufacturer. Iteration 3 fix: glycemic_load_per_serving restored to 0.0 (numeric) to satisfy Q4 requirement that glycemic_load_per_serving must be numeric; the validator rejected null as non-numeric for this field.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 0.0 to null, as per Veritas protocol for foods where GI is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrates.

Iteration 3: Restored glycemic_load_per_serving to 0.0 (numeric) after validator rejected null as non-numeric for this field. The Q4 validator requires glycemic_load_per_serving to be a numeric value. With carbohydrate per 28g serving being ~0.6g and GI not applicable, GL is effectively 0.0. Updated gi_source_match_notes and notes field to document this rationale.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Provolone Cheese record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The iteration 2 draft failed Q4 because glycemic_index was null but the validator required glycemic_index to be numeric. Analysis of the validator feedback clarifies the issue: glycemic_index must remain null per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods, but glycemic_load_per_serving must be numeric (0.0) rather than null. This iteration sets glycemic_load_per_serving to 0.0, which is the correct numeric representation of a clinically negligible glycemic load (~0.6g carbohydrate per 28g serving). All other sections are preserved from iteration 2. Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary, exact match) and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 173420 for fatty acid fractions and extended micronutrients. All three mandatory health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible. Aliases are comprehensive.
